cataloging_subject.label: The Siege of Thebes
cataloging_subject.scope_note: John Lydgate's Middle English verse romance The Siege of Thebes, including its 176-line return-pilgrimage prologue and three numbered parts. The intellectual work is distinct from its manuscript witnesses and early prints, Axel Erdmann's 1911 critical text and the posthumous 1930 apparatus volume completed by Eilert Ekwall, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, and all photographic or digital reproductions.

catalog_summary.title: The Siege of Thebes
catalog_summary.author: John Lydgate
catalog_summary.region: England; Thebes and the Canterbury return route as narrative settings

identifiers.scheme: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English

notes: The XML ends with the rubric 'Here endeth the destruction of Thebes'; Bodley 776 and Beinecke 661 also preserve this discovery form.

titles.value: Whan brighte Phebus passed was the Ram
Here endeth the destruction of Thebes

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: Benedictine monk of Bury St Edmunds; the work names him in a part-two closer and identifies him as a Canterbury pilgrim in its fictional frame.

agents.name: Axel Erdmann
notes: Editor of the 1911 critical text immediately represented by the U-M/CME encoding; the posthumous apparatus volume was completed by Eilert Ekwall in 1930.

scope.description: Middle English retelling of the Theban story from French prose, Latin mythographic, classical, biblical, and Chaucerian materials

notes: No single physical exemplar has been established; adaptation is used instead of asserting a direct translation from one surviving source copy.

scope.description: The 1911 critical text, including its marginal glosses and textual apparatus
Base text from British Library Arundel MS 119
Variants from twenty additional manuscripts and the two oldest printed editions

assertion.notes: The fictional return journey is astronomically dated to 27 April 1421, while Henry V's death on 31 August 1422 supplies the usual terminus ante quem.

notes: This dates the earliest presently identified manuscript witness, not composition itself.

date_statements.display: c. 1497, printed by Wynkyn de Worde

places.label: Canterbury to London return route
notes: Narrative frame rather than a place-of-composition claim.
No narrower composition place is asserted, and the localization of individual scribes is not transferred to the work.

scope.description: The legendary Theban history narrated inside the pilgrimage frame
The pilgrims' fictional homeward journey after Chaucer's Canterbury Tales

form_statements.description: Long narrative verse, predominantly in decasyllabic rhyming couplets, with a 176-line prologue and three numbered parts.

genre_statements.label: Classical romance
notes: The poem recasts legendary Theban history as a medieval romance of rulers, counsel, war, Fortune, and civic destruction.

genre_statements.label: Canterbury Tales continuation
notes: Lydgate presents the work as the first tale told on the pilgrims' return journey; this framing does not make it a work by Chaucer.

genre_statements.label: Mirror for princes
notes: Political counsel is integrated into romance rather than forming a freestanding conduct tract.

subject_statements.label: Thebes and the Seven against Thebes
Fratricide, civil war, and civic destruction

summaries.value: On the fictional homeward journey after Chaucer's Canterbury pilgrimage, Lydgate joins the company and is required by the Host to tell the first return tale. He recounts the foundation and destruction of Thebes: the history of Oedipus, the divided rule and fatal quarrel of Eteocles and Polynices, the embassy and exploits of Tydeus, the Seven against Thebes, fratricidal war, and the city's ruin. The poem repeatedly turns legendary history into counsel about prudent rule, truthful promises, peace, civic unity, and the destructiveness of private ambition.

Current scholarship reports thirty-one manuscripts, while Erdmann's 1911 edition used Arundel MS 119 as its base and collated twenty additional manuscripts plus the c. 1497 de Worde and 1561 Stow prints. Those historical witnesses and editorial layers are recorded in the paired lineage manifest; later discoveries do not retroactively become Erdmann's sources.

extent.description: The scholarly lineation runs 1-4716 across a 176-line prologue and three parts (177-1046, 1047-2552, and 2553-4716). The XML main stream contains 4,713 L elements because Erdmann's Arundel-based text skips numbered lines 389, 1062, and 1747; four variant lines for 1017-1020 are nested inside a footnote, so the raw file-wide count is 4,717 L. The file also contains 5 LG, 194 PB, 1,338 MILESTONE, 4,120 NOTE, and 5 P elements. These are representation metrics rather than universal manuscript extents.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: Lydgate dates and stages the homeward pilgrimage, meets Chaucer's company, describes himself and Canterbury, and accepts the Host's command to tell a tale.

content_parts.label: Part I
content_parts.summary: The tale begins beside the pilgrims and recounts the foundation of Thebes, its early rulers, and the history of Oedipus through the transition to his sons.

locators.value: pp. 10-44, lines 177-1046; numbered line 389 absent from the base-text stream

content_parts.label: Part II
content_parts.summary: The return-journey frame resumes near Boughton; the alternating kingship of Eteocles and Polynices fails, Tydeus carries an embassy and survives ambush, and preparations for war gather force.

locators.value: pp. 45-105, lines 1047-2552; numbered lines 1062 and 1747 absent from the base-text stream

content_parts.label: Part III
content_parts.summary: The Seven advance against Thebes, the war destroys its heroes and the rival brothers, the city falls, and a prayer for charity, peace, concord, and unity closes the poem.

related_works.title: Roman de Edipus
notes: The anonymous French prose Roman de Edipus is the poem's principal source, probably in a version not identical with the surviving printed text; no physical exemplar is identified.

related_works.title: Hystoire de Thebes
notes: A second French prose Theban redaction used alongside the principal Roman de Edipus tradition.

related_works.title: Genealogia deorum gentilium
notes: Boccaccio's Latin mythography is a major source for names and mythographic material.

related_works.title: The Canterbury Tales
notes: Lydgate supplies a fictional homeward sequel to Chaucer's pilgrimage, without implying Chaucerian authorship.

related_works.title: The Knight's Tale
notes: The Siege of Thebes supplies a Theban prehistory and thematic counterpart to Chaucer's tale.

Erdmann's historical edition key and current repositories are kept in the lineage manifest. Current scholarship reports thirty-one manuscripts, but only the twenty-one manuscripts and two early prints actually collated by Erdmann are upstream of the 1911 edition.

The XML includes marginal summaries, textual variants, and manuscript-leaf milestones inherited from Erdmann. They document the edition and encoding; they are not independent prose sections of Lydgate's poem.

The work's underlying medieval text is distinct from image licenses on manuscript photography. Cached whole-manuscript surrogates remain Git-ignored and their work folio/canvas portions are mapped separately.

open_questions.question: Which exact version or exemplar of the French Roman de Edipus did Lydgate use, and how did it interact with the Hystoire de Thebes and Latin sources?
next_steps: Review current source studies and compare the poem passage by passage with surviving French prose redactions without identifying a surviving copy as Lydgate's exemplar absent provenance evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the place and circumstances of composition be localized more narrowly than England without transferring an early witness's dialect or origin to Lydgate's authorial text?
next_steps: Review biographical, patronage, astronomical, and linguistic scholarship on the 1421-1422 composition window.

open_questions.question: Do Erdmann's skipped base-text numbers 389, 1062, and 1747 represent Arundel omissions, editorially reserved readings, or another explicit lineation convention in the 1930 apparatus?
next_steps: Inspect the corresponding Arundel leaves and Erdmann-Ekwall 1930 notes before assigning reconstructed text to any skipped number.
